title: APIs — How Programs Talk
subtitle: REST, JSON, endpoint design, and the rules of program-to-program communication

sections.title: What is an API?
sections.text:
An API is a set of rules for one program to talk to another. In web development, this usually means REST APIs — HTTP endpoints that accept requests and return structured data (usually JSON).

Think of it like a restaurant menu. The menu tells you what's available (endpoints), how to order (HTTP methods), and what you'll get back (the response). A well-designed API is intuitive — if you know how /users works, you can guess how /orders works.

REST uses nouns for resources and HTTP methods for verbs: GET /users lists them, POST /users creates one, DELETE /users/1 removes one. Resources are things. Methods are actions.

config.caption: REST API endpoints: resources as nouns, HTTP methods as verbs

sections.caption: REST APIs use nouns for resources and HTTP methods for actions
sections.code:
# A RESTful API for users
GET    /users          # List all users
POST   /users          # Create a user
GET    /users/1        # Get user with id=1
PUT    /users/1        # Replace user with id=1
PATCH  /users/1        # Update user with id=1
DELETE /users/1        # Delete user with id=1

# Response format
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Alice",
  "email": "alice@example.com"
}

sections.title: REST vs GraphQL
sections.text:
REST has multiple endpoints (/users, /users/1/orders). The server decides what data you get. Sometimes you get too much (over-fetching), sometimes not enough (under-fetching — need to make more requests).

GraphQL has one endpoint. You ask for exactly what you need: "give me the user's name and their last 3 orders." One request, no over-fetching. But it's more complex — you need a schema, resolvers, and careful performance tuning.

Which one? Start with REST. It's simpler and works for 90% of apps. Use GraphQL when different clients need different data shapes.

items: Client specifies exactly what it wants
One request can get nested data

sections.caption: REST vs GraphQL: different approaches to the same problem
sections.callout: Start with REST. It's simpler, well-understood, and works for 90% of applications. Move to GraphQL when you have multiple clients with divergent data needs.

sections.title: API Design Best Practices
sections.text:
Good API design is intuitive. Follow these rules and developers will thank you:

  • Nouns, not verbs — /users not /getUsers. Methods say what to do.
  • Right status codes — 201 for created, 404 for not found, 500 for server errors. Never return 200 for everything.
  • Version your API — /v1/users. Never break existing clients.
  • Paginate lists — ?page=2&limit=20. Always. Nobody wants 10,000 items at once.
  • Consistent errors — Always return { "error": "message", "code": "ERROR_TYPE" }.

Consistency is king. If one endpoint works one way, all should work that way.

items.heading: Naming
items.body: Plural nouns. /users, /orders. No verbs in URLs.

items.heading: Status Codes
items.body: 201 created, 204 deleted, 400 bad request, 404 not found, 500 server error.

items.heading: Error Format
items.body: Consistent { error, code } objects, never raw strings.

items.heading: Security
items.body: HTTPS always. Rate limit. Validate + sanitize all input.

sections.caption: Six principles of good API design — consistency is king
